era_context: The early 11th century CE continues to be a period of significant political, religious, and cultural change across Eurasia. The Fatimid Caliphate is at its height, the Song Dynasty is consolidating its power in China, the Chola Empire is expanding in South India, and feudal kingdoms are emerging in Europe. This is also a period of religious ferment, with the Christian Church approaching the Great Schism and Islamic scholarship flourishing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and the Americas (minimal written records)

events.title: Destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre (alternative dating)
events.description: Some Western Christian sources date the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre to 1009 CE rather than 1011 CE. This discrepancy reflects calendar conversion issues and possible confusion with earlier damage to the church. Islamic sources generally place the destruction in 1011 CE (401 AH), but Western chronicles sometimes cite 1009 CE. The destruction, whether in 1009 or 1011 CE, was ordered by Caliph al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah as part of his campaign of religious intolerance.

sources.name: Western Christian chronicles (e.g., Ademar of Chabannes)

events.certainty_note: The dating of the destruction is disputed between Western and Islamic sources. Western sources often cite 1009 CE, while Islamic sources place it in 1011 CE (401 AH). Modern scholarship generally favors the Islamic dating, but the discrepancy reflects calendar conversion challenges.
cross_references: Related to 1011-001 (destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, Islamic dating)

events.title: Sweyn II Estridsson becomes King of Denmark
events.description: Sweyn II Estridsson (also known as Sweyn II of Denmark) became King of Denmark around 1009 CE, following the death of his father Harald II. Sweyn II's reign is marked by conflicts with Cnut II (Canute the Great) of England and Norway over control of the North Sea empire. Sweyn II's rule represents a period of Danish resistance to Cnut's dominance and the assertion of Danish independence.
key_figures: Cnut II (rival for North Sea dominance)

sources.name: Icelandic sagas (Heimskringla by Snorri Sturluson, 13th century)

events.certainty_note: Sweyn II's accession is attested in Scandinavian sources, but the exact date is uncertain. 1009 CE is assigned by historiographic convention, though some sources place his accession slightly earlier or later.
cross_references: Contemporary with Cnut II's rise to power in England
Part of the North Sea power struggles (10th–11th centuries)

events.title: Famine and plague in Egypt and the Levant
events.description: Chronicles from Egypt and the Levant record severe famine and plague conditions in 1009 CE, attributed to the failure of the Nile inundation and disease outbreaks. The famine caused significant mortality and social upheaval throughout the Fatimid Caliphate. The crisis is attested in multiple Islamic sources and contributed to the economic and social pressures that characterized al-Hakim's reign.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Itti'az al-Hunafa bi-Akhbar al-A'imma al-Khulafa

events.certainty_note: Famine and plague are attested in Islamic sources for this period, though the precise dating and extent are sometimes uncertain. 1009 CE is assigned based on historiographic consensus.
cross_references: Part of broader environmental and demographic crises in the medieval Mediterranean (10th–11th centuries)

events.title: Zhenzong Emperor of Song China patronizes scholarship and the arts
events.description: Emperor Zhenzong of the Song Dynasty continued his patronage of scholarship, literature, and the arts in 1009 CE. Zhenzong's court became a center of intellectual activity, attracting scholars, poets, and artists from across China. The emperor's support for education and cultural development contributed to the flourishing of Song culture and the advancement of printing technology, which facilitated the dissemination of knowledge.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Song Shi (Official History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Zhenzong's patronage of scholarship and the arts is well-documented in Chinese historical records. The year 1009 CE falls within the middle of his reign (997–1022 CE).
cross_references: Part of the Song Dynasty's cultural and economic golden age (10th–11th centuries)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre's destruction is disputed between Western and Islamic sources. Western sources often cite 1009 CE, while Islamic sources place it in 1011 CE (401 AH). Modern scholarship generally favors the Islamic dating of 1011 CE, suggesting that Western sources may have confused the date or that the destruction occurred in 1011 CE but was reported in Western chronicles as 1009 CE. The discrepancy reflects calendar conversion challenges and the difficulty of synchronizing events across different calendar systems. Regarding Sweyn II's accession, some sources place it slightly earlier or later than 1009 CE, and the exact date is uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1009 CE is moderately rich for East Asia and the Fatimid Caliphate, but sparse for Northern Europe and the Americas. European sources are dominated by later sagas and chronicles, which are often unreliable for precise dating. Islamic sources provide good coverage of the Fatimid Caliphate and the Mediterranean region. Chinese sources are comprehensive for the Song Dynasty. The dating of events in this period is complicated by the use of multiple calendar systems and the conversion between them. The discrepancy between Western and Islamic dating of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre's destruction illustrates the challenges of synchronizing events across different calendar systems and the potential for confusion in later chronicles.

graph_edges.note: Western sources date the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre to 1009 CE, while Islamic sources place it in 1011 CE. The discrepancy reflects calendar conversion issues and possible confusion in later chronicles.

The famine and plague of 1009 CE contributed to the social and economic pressures that characterized al-Hakim's reign and may have influenced his religious policies, including the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in 1011 CE.
